labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The rise and subsequent decline in IgG levels over weeks can be used to explain development of immunity and the role of memory cells following infection.

parts.content: Describe the trend in antibody concentration over time after infection.
parts.markscheme:
- Antibody concentration starts at 0 $\mu$g mL$^{-1}$ at day 0.
- It increases from day 5 to day 20, peaking at 30 $\mu$g mL$^{-1}$.
- After day 20, antibody concentration declines (e.g. to 10 $\mu$g mL$^{-1}$ by day 40).

<marks>2 marks</marks> : <marks>1 mark</marks> increase to a peak (with time frame/data), <marks>1 mark</marks> decrease after the peak (with time frame/data)

parts.content: Explain how this data demonstrates the role of the immune system in defense against viral infections.
parts.markscheme:
- The adaptive immune response produces specific IgG antibodies after exposure to viral antigens.
- The delay to the peak (around day 20) reflects time needed for clonal selection/expansion and differentiation of B cells into plasma cells.
- After the peak, antibody concentration declines as antigen is cleared and short-lived plasma cells die; memory B cells persist enabling a faster secondary response.

<marks>2 marks</marks> : <marks>1 mark</marks> antibodies produced by B cells/adaptive response to antigen, <marks>1 mark</marks> time lag/peak explained by clonal expansion and/or memory/secondary response idea

questions.specification: The human body possesses complex biological mechanisms to prevent the entry of pathogens and to respond with high specificity when an infection occurs.

parts.content: Compare the roles of the skin and mucous membranes as the first line of defence against infectious diseases.
parts.markscheme:
- Skin acts as a continuous, tough physical barrier (keratinized layer) while mucous membranes are thinner and found at body openings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Skin produces sebum which lowers the pH to inhibit bacterial growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mucous membranes secrete sticky mucus to trap pathogens <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mucus often contains lysozyme, an enzyme that chemically destroys bacterial cell walls <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cilia on some mucous membranes move trapped pathogens out of the body (e.g., trachea) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award maximum 4 marks._

parts.content: Explain the sequence of events that prevents pathogens from entering the blood system following a laceration of the skin.
parts.markscheme:
- Blood vessels constrict at the site of damage to reduce blood loss <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Platelets adhere to exposed collagen at the wound site and form a temporary platelet plug <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Damaged tissues and platelets release clotting factors (thromboplastin) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calcium ions are required for activation of clotting factors in the cascade <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The inactive plasma protein prothrombin is converted into the active enzyme thrombin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Thrombin catalyzes the conversion of soluble fibrinogen into insoluble fibrin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fibrin forms a mesh/network of fibers that traps blood cells to form a semi-solid clot/scab, sealing the wound and reducing pathogen entry <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award maximum 4 marks._

parts.content: Explain how antibodies contribute to medical diagnostics.
parts.markscheme:
- Antibodies bind specifically to their antigen/epitope <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Antibodies can be immobilised on a solid support (e.g., membrane/well) to capture antigen from a sample <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A second antibody with a label (e.g., enzyme/fluorophore/coloured particle) can bind to form an antigen–antibody complex (sandwich assay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The label produces a detectable signal (colour change/fluorescence) indicating presence of the target molecule <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Quantity of signal can be proportional to antigen concentration (semi-quantitative/quantitative tests) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: pregnancy tests use antibodies to detect the hormone hCG in urine <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: antibodies are used in ELISA/rapid tests to diagnose infection by detecting pathogen antigens or patient antibodies <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using both the table and the graph, at what antigen dose does the antibody concentration plateau?
parts.markscheme: - About $5\text{–}10\ \mu$g mL$^{-1}$ (little/no further increase beyond $5\ \mu$g mL$^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare antibody concentrations on Day 3 and Day 7 across the antigen doses.
parts.markscheme:
- At every non-zero antigen dose, Day 7 antibody concentration is higher than Day 3 <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Antibody concentration increases with dose on both days, but Day 7 rises more/has a steeper increase at low–mid doses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The difference between Day 7 and Day 3 is small at very low dose and becomes larger as dose increases (up to the plateau) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both time points show a plateau at higher doses (little change from $5$ to $10\ \mu$g mL$^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using evidence from the data, explain the relationship between antigen dose and immune system activation.
parts.markscheme:
- Antibody concentration increases as antigen dose increases (dose-dependent response) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use of data to support (e.g. Day 7: $0.0$ at $0$ to $8.2$ at $5\ \mu$g mL$^{-1}$; Day 3: $0.0$ at $0$ to $5.8$ at $5\ \mu$g mL$^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher antigen dose increases probability/extent of B-cell activation and clonal expansion, leading to more plasma cells and antibody secretion <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Response approaches a maximum at high dose, suggesting limiting factors in activation/production <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Stated as a “dose-dependent immune response” with appropriate reference to increasing antibody concentrations with increasing dose <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Suggest a biological explanation for why the increase in antibody concentration slows beyond $5\ \mu$g mL$^{-1}$ of antigen.
parts.markscheme:
- Finite number of antigen-specific B cells / B-cell response becomes saturated (maximum clonal expansion/plasma cell output reached) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limiting helper T-cell signalling/cytokines or other resources (e.g. nutrients, space in lymphoid tissue) restrict further increase <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Negative feedback/immune regulation reduces further activation (e.g. regulatory T cells; antibody–antigen complexes reducing stimulation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Antigen processing/presentation becomes limiting at high antigen levels <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Memory cell formation shifts response away from further increasing short-term antibody concentration <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Antigens are essential for vaccine development. Suggest how the findings of this experiment could guide the formulation of vaccine doses.
parts.markscheme:
- Choose an antigen dose near the point just before/at the plateau (about $5\ \mu$g mL$^{-1}$) to achieve a strong antibody response <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Avoid higher doses because they produce minimal additional antibody but increase cost/antigen use <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Avoid unnecessarily high doses that could increase risk of adverse effects/overstimulation without added benefit <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one example in which antigens from red blood cells are involved in medical testing or safety.
parts.markscheme:
- Blood typing for transfusion safety

_**OR**_

- Rh testing in pregnancy <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept: Coombs test, cross-matching, etc.</note>

options.content:
| Soluble Protein | Catalytic Enzyme |
| :--- | :--- |
| Prothrombin | Thrombin |
options.markscheme: Prothrombin is the inactive precursor to thrombin, not the soluble protein that forms the final mesh.

options.content:
| Soluble Protein | Catalytic Enzyme |
| :--- | :--- |
| Fibrinogen | Thrombin |
options.markscheme: Thrombin is the active protease that catalyses the conversion of the soluble plasma protein fibrinogen into insoluble fibrin fibers, which form the structural mesh of a blood clot.

options.content:
| Soluble Protein | Catalytic Enzyme |
| :--- | :--- |
| Fibrin | Fibrinogen |
options.markscheme: Fibrin is the insoluble product of the reaction, and fibrinogen is the soluble substrate, not the enzyme.

options.content:
| Soluble Protein | Catalytic Enzyme |
| :--- | :--- |
| Thrombin | Vitamin K |
options.markscheme: Thrombin is an enzyme, not the substrate for the final mesh, and Vitamin K is a micronutrient required for the synthesis of clotting factors in the liver, not the catalyst for the final conversion.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question relates to how lymphocytes in the adaptive immune system cooperate to produce antibodies, which is visualized in the provided graph.

options.content: 0–15 days
options.markscheme: During the primary response, clonal expansion and differentiation of B cells occurs early after first exposure (lag/log phase), before peak antibody levels are reached.

options.content: 15–35 days
options.markscheme: This period is after the initial rapid clonal expansion; antibody levels are already high and then begin to decline.

options.content: 35–45 days
options.markscheme: This corresponds to the interval approaching the second exposure, not the early primary response when clonal expansion is most rapid.

options.content: $> 45$ days
options.markscheme: This is during the secondary response period, when memory cells drive a faster, larger antibody increase rather than primary clonal expansion.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C3.2 Defense against disease > C3.2.5 Infection control by phagocytes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C3.2.5 Infection control by phagocytes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks for the mechanism by which macrophages (a type of phagocyte) destroy pathogens, which is directly covered by the topic of infection control by phagocytes.

parts.content: Describe how macrophages destroy pathogens in the immune system.
parts.markscheme:
- Macrophages recognize and bind to pathogens using surface receptors. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Macrophages engulf pathogens through phagocytosis, forming a phagosome. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The phagosome fuses with a lysosome containing digestive enzymes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The enzymes break down/digest the pathogen's proteins and other components. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The digested material is expelled from the cell by exocytosis. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some pathogen fragments are presented on the cell surface as antigens to activate T-cells. <marks>1 mark</marks>
